texts.full_text: a six-lane toll road through the San Onofre State Beach park, a popular nature preserve known for its wildlife and world-class surf breaks. After a marathon hearing before a crowd of thousands, commissioners in Del Mar, Calif., voted 8 to 2 against the proposed Foothill South toll road, a six-lane, 16-mile extension to Route 241. The vote, which was greeted by an enormous cheer, followed 12 hours of public testimony from the crowd, of whom 2,500 made formal requests to speak, the largest number for a hearing in the commission's more than 30 years of operation. Some arrived by bus, brought by surf-industry companies opposed to the project. Others came as members of construction unions that support it. Some in the crowd carried surfboards as a symbol of protest. Others dressed as if attending a long-awaited football game. The noisy crowd sat in rows of chairs and banks of bleachers at the rear of Wyland Hall at Del Mar Fairgrounds to participate in a long-awaited showdown over the proposed Foothill South toll road project. The debate surrounding this issue has been intense, with environmentalists arguing that the highway would disrupt delicate ecosystems and increase pollution, while supporters claim it could alleviate traffic congestion on existing roads. During the hearing, speakers from various backgrounds testified about the potential impacts of the toll road. Environmental groups highlighted the threat to endangered species like the California gnatcatcher and the Coastal California least tern, as well as concerns over air quality and carbon emissions. They also pointed out that the project could harm recreational activities, particularly surfing, which is a significant economic driver for coastal communities. On the other side of the argument were supporters who emphasized the potential benefits for traffic flow and regional connectivity, noting that the current road system is insufficient to handle the growing population and daily commuting needs of commuters in Orange County and northern

texts.full_text: 'Thinking Allowed'Ralph Fevre and Amanda Robinson’s appearance on the BBC Radio Four show, Thinking Allowed with Laurie Taylor (24th October 2012). They discuss the key findings of Trouble at Work and particularLy the benefits of bringing sociological and criminological approaches to a problem that has, up to now, tended to be dominated by psychologists. They also commend on how these different perspectives can work together in practice. The episode delves into their research which highlights issues faced at various levels within the workplace environment – from individual level stress and mental health problems through organizational dysfunctionality up towards broader societal challenges such as discrimination, bullying or harassment. Fevre & Robinson emphasize that sociological theories provide insights on how social structures shape experiences of work-related troubles while criminology offers tools to address these issues by understanding deviance in the workplace context. Distribution These plants are found in various regions around the world. They can be seen growing wild or as ornamental trees on landscapes. 1) Northern Hemisphere: Species like S.auriculata grow primarily within temperate northern climates of Asia including China (Yunnan), North America, and Europe. 2）Southern hemispheres：South American countries are home to the majority species in this genus. Countries such as Brazil have a vast array with variations found between regions. 3) Tropical: Some like S.coriacea grow primarily within tropical climates of Africa (Ethiopia), Oceania, and Southeast Asia. Physiology Semecarpuses are medium to large-sized trees known for their attractive bark. The leaves often have a shiny appearance due in part from the presence or absence on hairs. 1) Leaves: They can be anywhere between 3-12 inches long depending upon species with an equal spread width at its widest point. 2）Bark：Semecarpus trees bear rough, grayish-brown bark that helps against pests and disease. A: First, I hope that you know that you shouldn't change module files directly, but to add hook function to your module and change that "hook" at beginning of function name with your module machine name. Second, you have to clear all the caches so Drupal will re-scan your module and figure out that there is new hook function and start using it. So, you have made changes in webform.api.php file but this isn't a right way. In fact, changing core files directly can lead into various issues such as site instability or security risks. The recommended approach would be to implement the hooks that are provided by Drupal itself using your custom module so you have full control over them and also avoid modifying any existing code in webform.api.php file. Here's how: 1. Create a new folder for your custom module (let’s say mymodule) inside modules/ directory of your drupal site. 2. Inside the newly created "mymodule" folder, create two files named .info.yml and mymodule.module respectively: - The first one is used to describe some basic information about our module such as its name, description etc., while - The second file contains all hooks functions that you want your custom module should implement. 3. Implement hook_webform_submission_presave in the newly created .module file. Here's an example of how it might look like: // mymodule.module / / function MYMODULE_webform_submission_presave(&$webform_submission, $webform) { ... } Remember to replace "MYMODULE" with your module machine name. After you've done this clear the cache by navigating to Configuration > Performance or /admin/config/development/performance and click on Purge all caches button. A: Essentially there was no difference – the LLRV was the "prototype", the LLTV the "production" version. Bearing new names, the LLTVs were nearly identical to the Dryden vehicles except for a few minor improvements to more closely match the LMs. (Dryden Flight Research Center - Luna(Dryden Flight Research Center - Lunar Landing Research Vehicle) The LLRV (Lunar Landing Research Vehicle) and LLTV (Lunar Landing Training Vehicle) were both crucial in preparing astronauts for the moon landings, but they did have some differences: 1. Purpose and Development: - LLRV: The LLRV was primarily a research vehicle designed to explore and test the feasibility of a vertical landing on the lunar surface. It was developed by NASA's Flight Research Center (now known as Armstrong Flight Research Center) in collaboration with Bell Aerosystems. - LLTV: The LLTV was a more refined and specialized version of the LLRV, specifically designed for training astronauts to perform the final descent and landing phase of the Apollo missions. It was built by Bell Aerosystems under NASA's direction. 2. Design Improvements: - Safety Features: The LLTV incorporated several safety improvements over the LLRV. For example, it had a more robust ejection seat system and better emergency procedures to ensure the pilot's safety. - Control Systems: The LLTV featured enhanced control systems that more accurately simulated the Lunar Module's (LM) dynamics. This included improved reaction control jets and a more sophisticated inertial measurement unit (IMU). - Structural Enhancements: The LLTV had a stronger and more durable structure to withstand the rigors of repeated training flights. texts.full_text: Specimens were assigned to two groups one had molar section with 10 specimens whereas other had premolar with remaining 10 specimens. Each group was placed on digital radiograph sensor (Schick CDR, size 2) together with aluminum step-wedge. Sensor was exposed to X-ray using standard technique. Two images were obtained for each group. Pixel measurements were made using NIH Image-J software. Mean pixel measurements were converted to optical density values and results compared between the two groups (molar vs premolars) with paired Student t-tests, as well assess interobserver reliability via intra-class correlation coefficients. In this study on radiopacity of resin cements for glass-ceramic-based restorations using digital imaging techniques. The goal was to establish a baseline understanding which resins can be used effectively in the cementation process without compromising image quality during diagnostic examination like X-rays or other form tomography scans when monitoring such complex indirect dental reconstructions. The samples consisted of 20 resin-cement specimens with ten from molar and premolars respectively. The use aluminum step-wedge provided a reference point for comparison to determine radiographic densities across different cements in the study groups, thus enabling an objective measure based on standardized exposure conditions that are applicable not only within clinical dental practices but also under research laboratory settings. Upon analysis using Image-J software (a tool commonly employed due its reliability and simplicity of use), two images were measured for each group to compute pixel values which could then be translated into optical densities — a crucial step when interpreting radiographs in terms that are clinically meaningful. Optical density, as it applies here is an indirect measure indicating the level at which light has been absorbed by different materials within this experimental set up; hence higher opaqueness correlates

texts.full_text: 2004. On 27 October 2010, Luigi Macaluso unexpectedly died at age 62 after suffering a heart attack. Luigi was married to Monica and was the father of four children, Stefano, Massimo, Anna and Margherita. More than 10 years ago, his two sons joined him at the head of the Sowind Group. An architect, former motor racing driver and entrepreneur, he had been behind the wheel of Girard-Perregaux since 1992. A firm believer in the value of an authentic Haute Horlogerie Manufacture, he pioneered the development of an integrated production strategy. A charismatic leader, he was able to make Sowind Group an essential player in Swiss Haute Horlogerie. Distinctions In 1998, Luigi Macaluso was awarded the Prix Gaïa for the entrepreneurship category, the highest distinction within the Swiss watchmaking world, attributed by the Musée International de l'Horlogerie. In 2008, he was appointed a Grand Officer of the Order of Merit of the Italian Republic. In 2009, he was appointed a Knight of the Order of Merit for Labour. texts.full_text: the larger geographical literature, and specifically that of cultural geography, has identified the spiritual aspects of landscapes/places, and in so doing, has highlighted their importance for both secular groups and broader society alike. The increased recognition of the positive relationship between being spiritually active and health begs the examination of spiritual places reputed for their healing and healthful qualities. This paper explores the spiritual and therapeutic dimensions of St. Anne de Beaupre, a site of pilgrimage and healing in Quebec, Canada. This location, renowned for its spiritual significance and the reported healing powers of its shrine, serves as a critical case study for understanding the interplay between spiritual landscapes and health outcomes. St. Anne de Beaupre, located on the shores of the St. Lawrence River, has a long history of attracting pilgrims seeking healing and spiritual renewal. The shrine, dedicated to St. Anne, the grandmother of Jesus, has been a focal point for Catholic devotion and healing practices for over 350 years. The site's therapeutic landscape is not only defined by the shrine itself but also by the surrounding natural environment, which is perceived to have a restorative and healing effect on visitors. ### Key Aspects of the Therapeutic Landscape 1. Spiritual Significance and Rituals: The shrine and its associated rituals, such as the touching of relics, the lighting of candles, and the participation in Mass, are central to the healing experience. These practices are believed to facilitate a connection between the divine and the individual, which is essential for spiritual and physical healing. 2. Natural Environment: The physical setting of St. Anne de Beaupre, including the surrounding forests, the St. Lawrence River, and the nearby Montmorency Falls, contributes to the therapeutic atmosphere. The natural beauty and tranquility of the environment are thought to promote relaxation, reduce stress, and enhance the overall well-being of visitors. 3. Wear Appropriate PPE (Personal Protective Equipment): Usegloves made of materials resistant to the solvents you are working with (such as nitrile or neoprene). Safety goggles or a face shield, and chemical cartridge respirator or air-linerespirator as indicated earlier, are essential. 2. Work in Well-Ventilated Areas: Open windows or use exhaust fans toreduce vapor levels in the work area. Avoid enclosed spaces unless they are properly ventilated, with air exchanges at least every three minutes. 3. Avoid Sources of Ignition: Keep matches, lighted cigarettes, and open flames away from the work area. Do not wear static-prone clothing that might generate sparks. 4. Label Containers Clearly: Ensure all containers of solvents areclearly labeled with their contents, hazard warnings, and safehandling instructions. 5. Manage Spills Promptly: Have absorbent materials on hand to clean up spills immediately and properly dispose of them according to local regulations. 6. Handle with Care: Use funnels or pour spouts when dispensing solvents to minimize spills. Work over trays lined with absorbent paper to catch drips. 7. Avoid Mixing Solvents: Blending different solvents can create dangerous conditions, such as flammable vapor mixtures or toxic fumes. 8. Dispose of Wastes Responsibly: Properly dispose of used solvents and rags according to all applicable local, state,and federal regulations. texts.full_text: after resection. All patients undergoing pancreatic surgery in our department between January 2008 and December 2014 were included. All complications occurring during hospitalization or in the 90-day period after discharge were documented. The hospital costs were analyzed and predictive factors of increased hospital costs were determined. One hundred and twenty seven patients wTo analyze the relationship between complications and hospital costs in the context of pancreatic resections in France, the study first segmented the costs of hospitalization into various categories. These categories included, but were not limited to, the costs of procedures, hospitalization, medications, and diagnostic tests. Upon analyzing the data, the study identified several complications that significantly increased the hospital costs. These complications included surgical site infections, the need for additional procedures such as reoperation, and the development of acute complications like pancreatic fistula and intra-abdominal abscess. To determine the predictors of increased costs, the study employed statistical methods such as logistic regression, adjusting for confounders. factors such as age, gender, and the presence of comorbidities, as well as the complexity of the surgical procedure and the surgical team's experience, were considered. The results of the study highlighted the substantial economic impact of post-surgical complications, revealing that the presence of complications could nearly double the hospital costs. The study underscored the importance of meticulous surgical planning, the optimization of post-surgical care, and the implementation of protocols for the early detection and management of complications. texts.full_text: ugh, secure without their weapons? The truth may be uncomfortable. It might require us confronting the root causes that have led individuals and communities towards gun ownership—be they fear-based or driven by misguided beliefs about personal freedom. But isn't a society where guns are no longer seen as essential to everyday life worth this discomfort? A world in which our children can grow up without being taught "safety rules" for handling firearms, but instead learn how best we might resolve conflicts and disagreements peacefully? The path towards such change won’t be easy. It will require a collective effort from all corners of society—from lawmakers to educators; community leaders as well individuals in their homes. But the conversation must start somewhere if America is serious about reducing gun violence, saving lives while preserving its cherished freedoms and values. Let's talk less around guns but more towards each other. Let’s not be fenced by our fears or misconceptions—instead let us build a future where we can coexist without the constant threat of deadly force. For example: ```java public class ReusableByteArrayOutputStream extends ByteArrayOutputStream { public void reset() { this.buf = new byte[buf.length]; // Reuse size to avoid realloc count = 0; } } ``` - Similarly, avoid calling `StringBuffer.toString()` or `getChars()` frequently, as these create new `String` objects. Instead, manage character arrays directly. 3. Object Pooling: - Implement object pools for frequently used objects (e.g., `Socket`, `InputStream`, or custom data strUctures). This reduces allocation pressure on the GC. - Use the Flyweight design pattern to share common immutable objects (e.g., strings, constants). 4. Avoid Finalizers: - Finalizers (`finalize()`) can fragment memory and delay GC in some VMs. Instead, manually manage resource cleanup in dedicated methods. 5. The suggestion to field B teams, or reserve squads, from the top Premier League clubs in this competition is met with significant skepticism. The primary concerns are that it could undermine the competition’s integrity, reduce the quality of the matches, and fail to contribute to the development of young talent in a meaningful way. Furthermore, the idea that this strategy could somehow translate to success in the England national team’s pursuit of World Cup glory is overly simplistic and not supported by evidence. The focus should instead be on nurturing young talent through the club’s academy system and providing them with a clear pathway to the first team, ensuring they gain the necessary experience and development to excel at the highest levels.

texts.full_text: er Bookmark Federal Energy Management Program: Federal Requirements for Renewable Energy on Google Bookmark Federal Energy Management Program: Federal Requirements for Renewable Energy on Delicious Rank Federal Energy Management Program: Federal Requirements for Renewable Energy on Digg Find More places to share Federal Energy Management Program: Federal Requirements for Renewable Energy on AddThis.com The Federal Energy Management Program (FEMP) is a comprehensive program designed to help federal agencies improve energy efficiency and integrate renewable energy sources into their operations. The primary goal of FEMP is to reduce the overall cost of energy for the government while promoting environmental sustainability. Here are some key aspects of the Federal Requirements for Renewable Energy within this framework: ### Key Components of Federal Requirements for Renewable Energy 1. Renewable Electricity Standards: Agencies must ensure that a certain percentage of their electricity consumption comes from renewable sources such as solar, wind, hydroelectric, and geothermal power. This is often mandated by federal laws like the Federal Energy Independence Security Act (EISA) of 2007. 2. Renewable Fuel Standards: Similar to electricity standards, agencies are required to use a specified amount of renewable fuels for their vehicles and other equipment that run on fuel. This includes biofuels like ethanol or biodiesel. 3. Energy Efficiency Measures: In addition to using renewable energy sources, federal facilities must implement measures to improve overall energy efficiency. This can include upgrading lighting systems, installing more efficient HVAC (heating, ventilation, and air conditioning) units, and optimizing building insulation. 4. Reporting Requirements: Agencies are required to report their energy consumption data regularly. These reports help track progress towards renewable energy goals and identify areas where improvements can be made. 5. texts.full_text: ady seen, it can be fatal. In a densely populated city like Conakry, disease spreads quickly," said UNICEF Representative in Guinea Dr. Mohamed Ayoya. The Government of Guinea, UNICEF, the World Health Organization (WHO) and Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) are joining forces to set up coordinated mechanisms to contain the outbreak. For the initial response, UNICEF will provide vaccines, refrigerators, needles, and other medical supplies and logistical support to the Government for the vaccination of children in the Kaloum and Dixinn neighborhoods of Conakry, as well as in the affected areas outside the capital. MSF and WHO will support vaccination efforts in the outbreak-declared areas of Conakry –namely Matam, Matoto, and Ratoma. The vaccination phase of the national campaign will begin in the weeks as soon as vaccines, supplies and funding to ensure a continuous rollout are available. Additionally, UNICEF and its partners will supply the Government with medicine to treat those who have already been infected by measles. "There is no time to waste," said Felix Ackebo, UNICEF Deputy Representative. "We need to move faster than the disease. Because measles takes up to 12 days to reveal its symptoms, it is that the disease has spread further into the country. All children who are still not immunized are at risk. Therefore, an outbreak immunization campaign is required as soon as possible." UNICEF and its partners are urgently seeking funding to replenish the stocks of vaccines needed to rollout the outbreak campaign across the country to vaccinate all children between nine months and 14 years. Additional medicines to treat those already infected are also required. # # # About UNICEF The United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) works in 190 countries and territories to save and improve children’s lives, providing health care and immunizations, water and sanitation, nutrition, education, emergency relief and more. The U.S. Fund for UNICEF supports UNICEF’s work through fundraising, advocacy, and education in the United States. texts.full_text: 2006 Fuzzy tactics aren’t helping the Mac community – The Unofficial Apple Weblog (TUAW) Posts like this that use underhanded techniques and shoddy math to prove a biased point aren’t helping the Mac community. In fact, they’re making it look even worse because, once found out, they are (rightfully) transformed into key evidence for clueless Apple fanaticism, which can easily harm the reputation of almost anyone with something genuinely educated and relevant to say about Apple or their products, whether it’s a good or *gasp* unpleasant statement. Unsurprisingly, the post that TUAW is refering to is from Roughly Drafted, which can only be described as the lunatic fringe of Mac fandom. While it’s perhaps understandable that some Mac users will want to “strike back” against what they see as media bias against the platform, as TUAW puts it: These wholly inaccurate and mThe article "Roughly Drafted’s not a good thing" by Ian Betteridge critiques the blog RoughlyDrafted (RD) for employing underhanded tactics and shoddy math in their posts about Apple, which he argues harms the Mac community. RD was perceived as part of the 'lunatic fringe' due to extreme views or aggressive arguments against media bias towardsApple products. Key points: 1. Tactics Used by RoughlyDrafted: They allegedly used deceptive data presentation—like cherry-picking statistics and ignoring counter-evidence—to make misleading claims, leading TUAW (The Unofficial Apple Weblog) to accuse them of poor methods that undermine the Mac community's image as educated tech enthusiasts. 2. Impact on Perceptions: - Such tactics made RD seem fanatical or clueless.
